summary: Cantrill reflects on attending a node.js meetup in Palo Alto, noting both the packed attendance and the surprisingly experienced demographic of attendees. He argues that node.js represents a genuine technological revolution rather than mere hype, evidenced by seasoned systems engineers who are rewriting existing applications in node. He identifies online social gaming as a natural early deployment space where node's performance characteristics provide decisive competitive advantages.
key_points: Node.js attracts experienced systems engineers, not just amateurs chasing trends
Online social gaming is emerging as a key deployment space for node.js applications
Seasoned developers are rewriting existing apps in node, not just building new ones
The quality of a technology community reflects the quality of the technology itself
money_quotes: Walking into a meetup and seeing one of the tribe tells you something immediate about not just the room, but the technology itself: that it is not mere syntactic sugar or iconoclasm for its own sake, but rather a true revolution in the way certain classes of systems are designed and built.

Node is not just an easier way of doing things (though that too, certainly) but rather that it offers such a vastly improved runtime that it merits reevaluation of systems that one has already built and deployed.
Online social gaming is a space in which node represents a decisive competitive advantage by offering the potential for much more interactive (and more social) gameplay.
Criticism that essentially amounts to discrediting node merely because of its newness or its popularity.
key_insight: The presence of experienced systems engineers adopting and rewriting production applications in a new technology is a stronger signal of its merit than any technical benchmark or hype cycle analysis.
